🌊 Western Lake Erie · 2025

Algal Bloom
Prediction & Monitoring

XGBoost + Gaussian Process interpolation for real-time chlorophyll-a forecasting across 8 monitoring stations in the Western Basin.

Test RMSE
—
µg/L prediction error
R² Score
—
variance explained
Test MAE
—
mean absolute error
Dataset Size
—
surface observations
About this project

Why Algal Bloom Prediction Matters

Harmful algal blooms (HABs) in Western Lake Erie threaten drinking water for 11 million people, close beaches, and cost the regional economy tens of millions of dollars annually. Early prediction is essential.

🧬

What We Measure

Chlorophyll-a (Chl-a) is the primary pigment in algae and the standard proxy for algal biomass. We predict Chl-a concentration in µg/L from 9 environmental variables including temperature, turbidity, dissolved oxygen, and phycocyanin pigment.

🤖

How We Model It

An XGBoost gradient-boosted regressor is trained via TimeSeriesSplit cross-validation and tuned with Optuna (60 trials). Gaussian Process spatial interpolation (Matérn 3/2 kernel) produces continuous severity maps from sparse point observations.

🔍

Interpretability

SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values reveal which features drive each prediction. Phycocyanin — a direct biomarker of cyanobacteria — emerges as the most influential predictor, followed by turbidity and water temperature.

Model Results

Prediction Performance

Trained on an 80/20 chronological split of weekly surface-water samples from 8 Lake Erie monitoring stations.

Observed vs Predicted

Held-out test set · dots colored by bloom severity

Points on the dashed line = perfect prediction. Most scatter falls within ±5 µg/L of the 1:1 line. Moderate R² of — reflects the genuine stochasticity of algal dynamics.

Feature Importance (SHAP)

Mean |SHAP value| across all training samples

Phycocyanin dominates — it's a direct biomarker of cyanobacteria. Turbidity and Water Temp are the next strongest drivers.
Algorithm Comparison

Why XGBoost?

Five models were evaluated with TimeSeriesSplit cross-validation (3 folds, chronological). All use median imputation and StandardScaler.

RMSE by Model (µg/L, lower = better)

TimeSeriesSplit CV mean ± std across 3 folds

Cross-Validation Results

All models · TimeSeriesSplit (3 folds)

Model RMSE (µg/L) R² MAE
XGBoost achieved a strong balance of CV and test performance. Random Forest was close in CV but XGBoost generalised better on held-out data.

Optuna-Tuned XGBoost Hyperparameters 60 trials

Monitoring Network

Western Basin Stations

8 stations operated by NOAA / Ohio EPA sample water quality weekly across the Western Basin of Lake Erie — the shallowest and most bloom-prone region of the lake.

Map data © OpenStreetMap contributors

Station Details

Weekly surface (0–1 m) water quality measurements

Variables measured: Water temperature, turbidity, dissolved oxygen, PAR (light), Secchi depth, phycocyanin, chlorophyll-a, microcystin, conductivity, wind speed.

Interpretation

Bloom Severity Guide

Chl-a thresholds based on WHO and US EPA water quality guidelines. The model predicts Chl-a in µg/L, which maps directly to these categories.

Low

< 10 µg/L

Normal background levels. Good water clarity, safe for recreation and drinking-water supply.

Moderate

10 – 30 µg/L

Elevated algal growth. Reduced water clarity. Possible mild taste and odour issues in drinking water.

High

30 – 50 µg/L

Visible bloom. Measurable microcystin toxin likely present. Beach advisories issued. Increased treatment costs.

Severe

> 50 µg/L

Dense bloom. High toxin risk. Beach closures. Potential drinking-water emergency (Toledo, 2014: >100 µg/L).

Context: In 2014, the Toledo water crisis resulted from microcystin levels exceeding safe limits. Chl-a exceeded 100 µg/L at affected intakes. This model targets early warning at the moderate–high boundary (~10–30 µg/L) to enable proactive response.

Western Lake Erie Algal Bloom Monitor

XGBoost · Gaussian Process Spatial Interpolation · SHAP Interpretability · Optuna Hyperparameter Optimisation
